read_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of change score with raw (pre-) score standardization (Morris ppc1 with Morris variance estimator)
read_imputemethod: r = 0.7 if not possible to recalculate
imbalance: randomized and nonrandomized trials included; ROB-2 (RCT) and ROBINS-I (non-RCT) for bias assessment.

I could perfectly recalculate the ES point estimate, but not the forest plot CI, because the aggregation procedure of the 3-level MA seems to change the variance estimates. But using the script, I could fully reproduce the originally calculated ES variance.

recalc_calcmethod: was able to recalculate the Effect size with the given equation (4) by Favro which is in line with Morris 2008. I was not able to recalculate CI / variance as the change SD was not given and r=0.7 led to different CI.

read_calcmethod: SMD of change score with raw score standardization (Morris)
read_imputemethod:
informative Gaussian prior centered at r = 0.85 : "Therefore, primary data obtained
from relevant studies (including those produced in the laboratories of the study team) were used to develop informative priors to model within study variances (Gaussian prior centred at r = 0.85 and range from approximately 0.70–0.99)"
imbalance: only RCTs included, ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments: It's unclear which of the Morris types was used. Also if the priors for r were only part of the variance estimation in the Bayesian MA, or also for the (non-MA) ES calculation for all studies. ES are shown as distributions under a Bayesian framework, so it is not possible to recalculate, since also no code and data for individual study ES is presented.
recalc_comments: Bayesian MA, so individual ES recalculation is not possible here.

read_calcmethod: Three-Level random-effects Bayesian hierarchical model to pool ES and model mean effects.
justification: No justification of ES calculation. Imputation of within-study variance was modelled by primary data obtained from relevant studies (reference to Morris, 2008)
imbalance: Cochrane RoB2
read_comments: This form of meta-analysis does not fit into our recalculation scheme as it is based on a Bayesian model.

read_calcmethod: MD of change scores (and change score sds)
read_imputemethod: r = 0.975-0.99 depending on the variable and based on the median values of one study that reported correlations
justification: correlation imputation based on reported values in one study and similar to the values in earlier MAs of the same group that used the same procedure
imbalance: only RCTs includede, ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments: The correlation imputation section was very detailed and well-explained. 0.99 seems very high though. The authors state that they published all data and analysis code, this may make the recalculation easy.
recalc_study: Spillane et al. 2009 (12 out of 12 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: MD (not assuming homoscedasticity)
recalc_imputemethod: 0.975 (in this instance, so exactly as the authors described it)
recalc_comments: I could fully reproduce the ES, but only when I multiplied the control group sample size, mean, and sd by 1/2. I think this was done because there are two intervention group for this particular study (though not in the same forest plot) and the MA authors used this to account for potential double-counting). But then one should halve the sample size and definitely not also the mean and sd. This can (and in this instance does) highly bias ES towards exaggerating treatment effects. I found this out by looking at the data sheet the MA authors provided on the OSF, so providing data and code here proved to be extremely helpful. (On another note, the reference number was incorrect in the forest plot)

read_calcmethod: weighted mean difference, SD of change score according to Cochrane Handbook

justification: imputation of correlation coefficient supported by previous studies
imbalance: Cochrane Risk Of Bias Tool (ROB2)
read_comments: Correlation coefficient is based on one study
recalc_study: Fig. 2- Study 12: Spillane et al. (2009)
recalc_calcmethod: MD based on change scores means and SD as reported
recalc_comments: The control group (n, mean and SD) was divided by 2, because the MA stated to split the control group sample proportionately based on the number of subjects in each intervention group, if a study comprised multiple eligible creatine interventions. I suppose, they made this because the study was included in forest trees of lean body mass and body fat. As these are both continuous outcomes, dividing the mean and SD does not make any sense, from my point of view, and results in a different ES (MA: 1.00; new: -0.15)

recalc_comments: I had troubles retrieving the original research studies underlying the MA. The first I could retrieve had raw data available, from which I could calculate the group means in the forest plot, but not the group sds. So there may be a data extraction error. Using the means and sds from the forest plot I could reproduce the ES. For the second study I could receive I had another issue, as I could match the mean and sd of the intervention but not the control group (so maybe another extraction error). For the third study everything worked, when imputing a pre-post correlation of r = 0.5.

read_calcmethod: SMD of within-group SMD, both with Hedges' correction and the same variance estimator
read_imputemethod: unclear, based on available data (but unclear what if not enough data is reported)
justification: Lakens 2013 for calculating the within group ES and using the Hedges' correction, Kebede et al. 2019 for calculating the correlation from reported sds, Madeyski & Kitchenham 2018 for the between group SMD, Goulet-Pelletier & Cousineau (2018) for the variance estimator
imbalance: only crossover trials included, self-developed checklist for bias-assessment
read_comments: The procedure is well-explained, well-referenced, and the appendix provides all formula used. I am unsure whether the used approach is mathematically correct, because (1) the variance estimator is for independent groups but also used for the within group ES here (2) there is an error (typo?) in the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation (3) the practice of standardizing a difference of standardized means may yield results that are different to interpret (4) The cross-over nature of the trials is not fully taken into account (e.g., not using the between condition correlation).
recalc_study: Jones et al. 2008 (9 out of 11 from Fig 2A)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on within-group SMD (both with Hedges' correction and both with an independent group variance estimator).
recalc_comments:
I could finally perfectly recalculate a given effect size, but only when following the mistakes from the MA analysis: 
1. the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation is not just incorrectly applied but also contains an error, with the result that a correlation of r = 0.5 is always imputed
2. the variance formula (which is inappropriate for the within group design) also has an error in the calculation of the degrees of freedom, as has the Hedges' correction factor
3. The standardization procedure of the between group ES standardizes already standardized SMDs by raw score SDs, which will almost always leads to extremely small ES (but large CIs)
4. The between group ES does not take the crossover design into account but treats the am and pm groups as independent (loss of power)

In the end, I could only successfully recalculate a single ES (I'll upload this file as the "original study pdf").

recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of post scores and their sd (with Borenstein variance estimator)
recalc_comments: I couldn't match the ES of the first study (the study only reported change scores and no post scores), but the recalculation worked for the second (which I upload here). The MA authors use the Borenstein variance estimator, despite stating that they used metafor for the MA. This is rather uncommon, because that estimator is not the default setting in metafor. Maybe the ES calculation was done with another software.
